section.title: Final Instructions and Greetings
section.sectionRationale: The apostle's last recorded words are intensely personal: a longing for Timothy to come, the names of those who left and those who stayed, a cloak and the parchments, and a warning about Alexander. Yet at the heart of the loneliness stands the great confession of the letter's end. When no one stood with Paul, the Lord stood by him and strengthened him, delivered him from the lion's mouth, and would bring him safely into His heavenly kingdom. The letter closes as it must, with grace.
section.thesis: Deserted by some and cherishing the few who remained, Paul asks Timothy to come and entrusts final instructions; but the ground of his hope is that when no one stood with him the Lord stood by him, delivered him, and would bring him safely into His heavenly kingdom, to whom be the glory forever.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the call to come, with those who deserted and those who stayed; the warning about Alexander and the lonely first defense; the Lord who stood by Paul and will bring him safely into His heavenly kingdom; and the closing greetings and grace.

units.label: Come to me
units.note: those who left, those who stayed

verses.text: Make every effort to come to me quickly,
because Demas, in his love of {{aion|this world}}, has deserted me and gone to Thessalonica. Crescens has gone to Galatia, and Titus to Dalmatia.
Only Luke is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you, because he is {{euchrestos|useful}} to me in the ministry.
Tychicus, however, I have sent to Ephesus.
When you come, bring the cloak that I left with Carpus at Troas, and my scrolls, especially the {{membranai|parchments}}.
Alexander the coppersmith did great harm to me. The Lord will repay him according to his deeds.
You too should beware of him, for he has vigorously opposed our message.
At my first defense, no one stood with me, but everyone deserted me. May it not be charged against them.

But the Lord {{paristemi|stood by}} me and {{endynamoo|strengthened}} me, so that through me the {{kerygma|message}} would be fully proclaimed, and all the Gentiles would hear it. So I was delivered from the mouth of the lion.
And the Lord will {{rhyomai|rescue}} me from every evil action and bring me safely into His {{epouranios|heavenly}} kingdom. To Him be the glory forever and ever. Amen.
Greet Prisca and Aquila, as well as the household of Onesiphorus.
Erastus has remained at Corinth, and Trophimus I left sick in Miletus.
Make every effort to come to me before winter. Eubulus sends you greetings, as do Pudens, Linus, Claudia, and all the brothers.
The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all.

aion.gloss: An age, an era, the present world order in its passing form. Demas deserted Paul because he loved this present age, the world as it now is rather than the age to come. His tragedy is not a single sin but a settled affection: he set his heart on what is fading instead of on Christ's appearing, and so let go of the apostle and the gospel.

euchrestos.gloss: Useful, serviceable, profitable for good use. Paul once refused to take Mark on a journey, yet now, years later, calls him useful for ministry. The word marks a restored and proven servant, showing that failure need not be final and that the Lord makes the once-rejected fruitful again in His work.

membranai.gloss: Parchments, prepared animal-skin sheets used for the most valued and durable writings, a Latin loanword. Facing death, Paul still wants his books and especially the parchments, most likely portions of Scripture. Even at the end the apostle hungers for the word of God, showing that no maturity outgrows the need to keep reading and being shaped by Scripture.

paristemi.gloss: To stand by, to come to one's side, to be present as a helper. When every human supporter deserted Paul at his defense, the Lord Himself took the place beside him that no one else would take. The word paints a courtroom scene in which the risen Christ stands as the advocate and companion of His servant when all others are gone.

endynamoo.gloss: To put power into, to strengthen from within. The Lord did not merely stand near Paul; He infused him with strength for the task. The same power that raised Christ sustains His servant, so that the message could be proclaimed even from the dock, and human weakness became the stage for divine strength.

kerygma.gloss: The proclamation, the thing preached, the announced message of the gospel. Paul's chief concern at his trial was not his own safety but that the kerygma be fully proclaimed so all the Gentiles might hear. Even in chains the gospel was not bound; God used the apostle's danger to carry the message further, showing that the message matters more than the messenger's comfort.

rhyomai.gloss: To rescue, to deliver, to draw someone to oneself out of danger. Paul is certain the Lord will rescue him from every evil deed, not by sparing his life but by bringing him safely through death into the heavenly kingdom. The rescue he expects is final and complete: not escape from dying but deliverance into glory.

epouranios.gloss: Heavenly, belonging to heaven, above. The kingdom into which Paul expects to be brought is not of this passing age but the heavenly kingdom of Christ. Set against Demas who loved this present world, Paul's hope is fixed on the world above, so that death is the doorway into the kingdom that cannot fade.

basic.context: These are the closing lines of Paul's last letter. He urges Timothy to come to him soon, because he is largely alone: Demas has abandoned him out of love for the world, others have gone on assignments, and only Luke remains. He asks Timothy to bring Mark, and to bring his cloak and his books, especially the parchments. He warns about Alexander, who did him great harm, and recalls that at his first trial no one supported him. But then comes the high point: the Lord stood by him, strengthened him so the gospel could be fully preached, delivered him, and will bring him safely into the heavenly kingdom. Paul ends with greetings and the blessing of grace.
basic.hermeneutics: Even the most personal parts of Scripture teach us. The names and requests are real history, but they reveal how the gospel holds a man at the end of his life. The lesson is not that we must fetch a cloak, but that Christ stands by His people when everyone else fails, that He is faithful to bring them home, and that His grace is the last word.
basic.originalLanguages: Demas loved the aion, this present age. Mark is now euchrestos, useful, for ministry. Paul wants his membranai, the parchments. When all deserted him, the Lord did paristemi, stood by him, and did endynamoo, strengthened him, so the kerygma, the proclamation, would be fully made. The Lord will rhyomai, rescue him, into His epouranios, heavenly, kingdom.
basic.typology: Paul's deliverance from the mouth of the lion recalls God's rescue of His servants like Daniel, and points to the final deliverance of all the faithful. The advocate who stands beside the accused foreshadows Christ our advocate before the Father.
basic.christ: Christ is the Lord who stood by Paul when no one else would, the strength by which the gospel was proclaimed, and the King whose heavenly kingdom is Paul's sure destination. He is the faithful friend of the deserted and the rescuer who brings His servants safely home through death.
basic.god: God receives the glory forever as the one who never abandons His servant. When human faithfulness fails, His does not. He strengthens the weak, uses their trials to spread His word, and guarantees their arrival in His kingdom, so the last note of the letter is doxology and grace.
basic.crossReferences: Second Timothy 1 remembers Onesiphorus, who was not ashamed of Paul's chains. Psalm 22 speaks of being saved from the mouth of the lion. Matthew 28 promises Christ's presence to the end, the promise Paul proves true here.
basic.oneStory: God has always kept His people to the end, standing with them when the world deserts them and bringing them safely home. Paul's lonely cell becomes a window on that faithfulness: the Lord stands by His servant, carries the gospel forward through him, and welcomes him into the heavenly kingdom. The story of redemption ends not in the desertion of friends but in the presence of Christ and the triumph of grace.

context.literary: The movement is a personal postscript, yet it is carefully shaped. It moves from urgent request, come to me, through the roll call of those who left and those who stayed, to the warning about Alexander, and then rises suddenly to the confession of verses 17 and 18, the Lord stood by me, capped by a doxology. The closing greetings and the final blessing of grace round off not only this movement but the whole letter and, in effect, Paul's recorded ministry.
context.historical: Paul is a prisoner in Rome awaiting execution, and travel is difficult, hence the plea to come before winter when the seas closed. The cloak matters against the cold of a dungeon; the parchments were costly and precious. The first defense refers to an initial hearing where, in a dangerous political climate, no friend dared to appear. The mouth of the lion may point to Nero, to execution, or to Satan's assault; either way Paul was spared for a time so the gospel could sound out.
context.canonical: As the closing of Paul's final letter, these verses form the last words of the great apostle in the New Testament. The confession that the Lord stands by His servant and will bring him safely into the heavenly kingdom has comforted the church ever since, and the letter fittingly ends where all Paul's letters end, with grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To draw Timothy to his side, to settle final practical matters, and above all to leave the church a testimony that Christ is faithful to the end: when human support fails, the Lord Himself stands by His servant, uses his trials for the gospel, and brings him safely home, so that the letter closes in confidence, doxology, and grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The personal requests and the list of names are descriptive history. The abiding truths they carry are prescriptive and doxological: the danger of loving this world, the value of restored servants and of Scripture, the certainty that Christ stands by His people, and the fitting response of glory to God and reliance on His grace.

debates.question: What is the mouth of the lion from which Paul was delivered?

views.label: A figure for deadly danger or Nero
views.case: The phrase pictures the extreme peril of Paul's trial, possibly the emperor himself, from which he was spared so the gospel could be proclaimed.

views.label: The assault of Satan
views.case: Scripture calls the devil a roaring lion, so some see Paul rescued from a spiritual attack that aimed to silence him.

debates.bearing: Either way the deliverance is the Lord's doing, granting Paul time to see the gospel fully proclaimed before his departure.

debates.question: How can Paul both pray Alexander be repaid and pray his deserters be forgiven?

views.label: A prayer left to God's justice
views.case: Paul does not take revenge but entrusts Alexander's active opposition to the righteous Judge, while asking mercy for those who merely failed him in fear.

views.label: A distinction between hardened enemy and weak friends
views.case: Alexander vigorously opposed the message, so judgment is invoked, whereas the deserters were frightened brothers for whom Paul seeks pardon.

debates.bearing: Both readings show Paul refusing personal vengeance, leaving justice to God while extending mercy, in step with Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The movement turns on a cluster of vivid words. Demas deserted Paul because he loved the aion, this present age, the fading world set against the age to come. Mark, once refused, is now euchrestos, useful, a restored and proven servant. Facing death, Paul still asks for the membranai, the parchments, most likely Scripture, for even the apostle never outgrows the word. At his defense every friend fled, but the Lord did paristemi, He stood beside Paul as an advocate takes his place beside the accused, and did endynamoo, He put strength into him from within, so that the kerygma, the gospel proclamation, would be fully made and all the Gentiles hear. Paul is sure the Lord will rhyomai him, rescue and draw him out of every evil, not by sparing his life but by bringing him into the epouranios kingdom, the heavenly kingdom above, where the one who loved this world will not go but the one who loved Christ's appearing will.

typology.type: Deliverance from the mouth of the lion
typology.fulfillment: The Lord's rescue of His faithful servant, in the pattern of Daniel spared among the lions, pointing to the final deliverance of all the saints.

typology.type: The Lord standing by the accused when all desert him
typology.fulfillment: Christ the advocate who stands with and pleads for His people, never leaving them alone in their trial.

study.christ: Christ is the Lord who stood by Paul when every human friend deserted him, the strength poured into a weak and lonely man so that the gospel could still be fully preached. He is the deliverer from the lion's mouth and the King of the heavenly kingdom into which He will bring His servant safely through death. Set against Demas, who loved this world and let go of Christ, Paul clings to the Lord who never lets go of him. The whole movement quietly displays Christ as the faithful friend of the forsaken, the advocate who takes His place beside the accused, and the Savior whose grace is the letter's final word.
study.god: God is faithful when people are faithless, standing by His servant when all others flee, and He is glorified as such: to Him be the glory forever and ever. He turns Paul's trial into a platform for the gospel, strengthens the weak from within, and guarantees the safe arrival of His people in the heavenly kingdom. He leaves justice for opposition in His own righteous hands while extending mercy to frightened friends. Such a God is worthy of the doxology that closes the letter, and His grace is the blessing Paul leaves with Timothy and the church.

crossReferences.note: Others turned away from Paul, but Onesiphorus was not ashamed of his chains and often refreshed him, the kind of faithfulness prized here.
Save me from the mouth of the lion, the language Paul echoes of the Lord's deliverance.
Christ's promise to be with His people always, to the end of the age, proved true when the Lord stood by Paul.
Do not love the world, for the world is passing away, illuminating why Demas's love of this present age was a desertion of Christ.

study.oneStory: God keeps His people to the very end, and Paul's last words prove it. Deserted by friends and facing the sword, the apostle finds the Lord standing beside him, strengthening him, spreading the gospel through his trial, and promising to bring him safely into the heavenly kingdom. The long story of redemption, from a garden lost to a kingdom gained, closes here on a note of faithfulness and grace: the world that Demas loved is passing away, but the servant who loved Christ's appearing is carried home. To Him be the glory forever and ever.

sermon.title: The Lion's Mouth
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds 2 Timothy 4:14-18, showing how, when no one stood with Paul, the Lord stood by him and strengthened him, delivered him from the lion's mouth, and would bring him safely into His heavenly kingdom.

options.text: Because he loved this present world.
options.note: Verse 10 names his love of this present age as the cause of his desertion.

options.text: Because Paul had wronged him personally.
options.note: The text gives no such reason; the cause is love of the world.

options.text: Because he was sent away on gospel work like Tychicus.
options.note: Others left on assignment, but Demas deserted out of love for the world.

options.text: Because his heart was set on the world rather than Christ's appearing.
options.note: True and close, restating his love of this passing age.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about Mark in this passage?

options.text: That he is useful for ministry and should be brought along.
options.note: Verse 11 calls Mark useful and asks Timothy to bring him.

options.text: That he can never be trusted again after failing before.
options.note: The opposite: Paul now calls the once-refused Mark useful.

options.text: That he has deserted Paul like Demas.
options.note: That was Demas; Mark is commended as useful.

options.text: That a servant who once failed has been restored and made fruitful.
options.note: True and close, drawing out the meaning of Mark now being useful.

quiz.q: What happened at Paul's first defense?

options.text: No one stood with him; everyone deserted him.
options.note: Verse 16 records that all deserted him at his first defense.

options.text: The whole church rallied publicly to his side.
options.note: The opposite is true; everyone deserted him.

options.text: He was immediately released and all charges dropped.
options.note: The point is his abandonment by people, not the verdict.

options.text: He was left alone by those who should have supported him.
options.note: True and close, restating that everyone deserted him.

quiz.q: What is the heart of Paul's confidence in verses 17 and 18?

options.text: The Lord stood by him and will bring him safely into His heavenly kingdom.
options.note: Verses 17 and 18 rest on the Lord's presence, strength, and final deliverance.

options.text: That he would surely escape execution and live on.
options.note: His hope is deliverance into the heavenly kingdom, not escape from death.

options.text: That his own courage had never once wavered.
options.note: The confidence is in the Lord who stood by him, not in himself.

options.text: That Christ is faithful to strengthen and finally save His servant.
options.note: True and close, summarizing the Lord standing by and rescuing him.

quiz.q: How does Paul respond to being rescued and kept by the Lord?

options.text: With a doxology: to Him be the glory forever and ever.
options.note: Verse 18 answers the Lord's faithfulness with glory to God.

options.text: With boasting in his own endurance.
options.note: He gives glory to the Lord, not to himself.

options.text: With bitterness toward those who deserted him.
options.note: He asks that it not be charged against them and praises God instead.

options.text: By turning his rescue into praise of God.
options.note: True and close, capturing the doxology that flows from the Lord's faithfulness.

questions: Demas loved this present world and let go of Christ. Where is love for the world quietly pulling at your own heart, and what would it look like to set your hope on Christ's appearing instead?
Paul, once unwilling to take Mark, now calls him useful. Is there someone who failed you, or a way you failed others, where the Lord may be writing a story of restoration?
Even facing death, Paul wanted the Scriptures. What does your hunger for God's word reveal about your heart, and how might you return to it in this season?
When no one stood with Paul, the Lord stood by him. Where do you feel alone right now, and how does it change things to know Christ stands beside His people when others fall away?

Paul answered the Lord's faithfulness with glory to God and left Timothy with grace. As you finish this letter, what would it look like to entrust your own ending, whatever it holds, to the Lord who brings His people safely home?

translationNotes.note: Parchments renders a Latin loanword for prepared animal-skin sheets used for durable, valued writings, most likely portions of Scripture.

Stood by me translates a word for taking one's place beside another as a helper or advocate; the mouth of the lion is a figure for deadly peril, variously understood as Nero, execution, or Satan's assault.
Prisca is a variant of Priscilla; see Acts 18:2, the same couple who labored with Paul.
Some manuscripts (BYZ and TR) add Amen at the close; the BSB text ends with Grace be with you all.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 4:9-22. No dashes present in this movement's text; footnote markers for Prisca (v19) and the Amen variant (v22) were moved to translationNotes.
